narrative:
# EOD Macro-Risk Brief | 2026-07-03

**Headline:** Overall risk stable at 5.8, but developing-world debt crisis escalated to Elevated on higher US real yields.

**Stories with level changes:**
• **Developing-world debt crisis** (5→6, now Elevated): US 10y real yield rose 50bps to 2.25%, widening debt service costs for emerging markets and increasing refinancing pressure.

**Highest-risk watch:** Critical minerals as a weapon and AI power & grid bottleneck both remain at level 7 (High); the latter poses acute infrastructure constraints as build-out accelerates.

stories.name: Treasury basis trade (hidden leverage)

indicators.label: SOFR minus EFFR (%)
MOVE index (Treasury volatility)
SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)

stories.summary: Hedge funds run a highly leveraged arbitrage between Treasury cash bonds and futures. It is a major source of liquidity, but a spike in funding costs or Treasury volatility can force rapid unwinds, as briefly happened in March 2020 and again in 2025.

stories.name: Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade

indicators.label: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk)
30-year JGB yield (%)
Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)

stories.summary: Decades of near-zero rates made Japan the funding leg of a vast global carry trade. As the BoJ normalizes and JGB yields rise, fiscal financing gets harder and carry unwinds can transmit stress worldwide - the August 2024 mini-crash was a preview.

stories.name: Private credit's opaque expansion

indicators.label: US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)
Direct-lending default rate (%)
Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)

stories.summary: Lending has migrated from banks to lightly regulated private-credit funds, where assets are marked infrequently and leverage is opaque. Defaults are rising from a low base; the risk is that losses surface suddenly rather than being visible in real time.

stories.name: Developing-world debt crisis

indicators.label: Broad US dollar index (FRED DTWEXBGS)
US 10y real yield (TIPS, %)
New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A strong dollar, high real rates, and heavy foreign-currency debt loads leave many developing economies vulnerable to a sudden stop. A cluster of sovereign defaults or new IMF programs would signal the slow-burn crisis turning acute.

stories.name: China's price spillover (deflation or cost-push)

indicators.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
USD/CNY (yuan depreciation = higher = risk)
New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

stories.summary: China's factory-gate prices spill into global goods markets in BOTH directions: years of PPI deflation exported disinflation and squeezed competitors, while a sharp 2026 reversal to positive PPI (energy/commodity cost-push) now exports input-cost pressure. Either large swing is destabilising; the indicator is scored on the size of the move, not its sign.

stories.name: Critical minerals as a weapon

indicators.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)

stories.summary: China dominates the mining and especially the processing of rare earths and critical minerals, and has shown willingness to use export licensing as leverage. Supply chains for magnets and defense inputs have little near-term substitute.

stories.name: Stablecoins & Treasury demand

indicators.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Dollar stablecoins have become sizeable holders of short-term Treasuries, linking crypto plumbing to the funding market. A large redemption wave could force rapid T-bill selling; the GENIUS Act's implementation is the key regulatory variable.

stories.name: AI build-out circular financing

indicators.label: US investment-grade OAS (%) - big-issuer proxy
AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)

stories.summary: AI infrastructure spending vastly exceeds current AI revenue, increasingly financed with debt and circular vendor arrangements. If monetization disappoints or GPU-backed financing strains, the capex cycle could reverse sharply.

stories.name: CRE maturity wall / regional banks

indicators.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A wall of commercial real-estate loans - especially office - matures into higher rates and lower valuations, concentrated at regional banks. Rising delinquencies and 'extend and pretend' practices defer, rather than resolve, the losses.

stories.name: France / eurozone fragility

indicators.label: OAT-Bund 10y spread (%)
Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)

stories.summary: France combines a wide deficit, a fragmented parliament, and recurring budget standoffs, widening its spread over Germany. Political instability or a rating downgrade would test eurozone cohesion at its second-largest economy.

stories.name: Hidden consumer debt

indicators.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
US unemployment rate (%)
BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)

stories.summary: Beneath headline figures, subprime auto and buy-now-pay-later delinquencies are climbing, and the labor market is the swing factor. A rise in unemployment would convert stretched-but-current borrowers into defaults quickly.

stories.name: Gold / de-dollarization

indicators.label: Gold price (USD/oz)
USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)

stories.summary: Central banks are accumulating gold and building non-dollar settlement arrangements at the margin. It is a slow erosion rather than a sudden shift, but reserve 'weaponization' events can accelerate diversification away from the dollar.

stories.name: Insurance retreat

indicators.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
US single-family mortgage delinquency (%, FRED DRSFRMACBS)
Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)

stories.summary: Insurers are retreating from climate-exposed markets through non-renewals and exits, shifting risk onto homeowners and state backstops. Severe catastrophe seasons strain the system and feed into housing and mortgage stress.

stories.name: Pensions / demographics

indicators.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
Large public-pension funded ratio (%)

stories.summary: Aging demographics and underfunded public pensions create slow fiscal pressure, while leveraged liability-driven strategies can blow up fast - as the UK gilt/LDI crisis showed in 2022. Benefit-reform fights are the political pressure valve.

stories.name: AI power & the grid bottleneck

indicators.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)

stories.summary: Data-center electricity demand is colliding with multi-year grid interconnection queues and slow capacity additions. Whether this is an acute shortfall or eases depends on the pace of AI build-out versus new supply - forecasts diverge widely.

stories.name: Copper & the electrification deficit

indicators.label: Copper price (USD/lb, COMEX HG=F)
Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)

stories.summary: Electrification and AI infrastructure point to structural copper deficits as demand outruns new mine supply, which takes a decade to build. Price is the live signal; mine disruptions and inventory draws would confirm tightening.

stories.name: Water - the underpriced input

indicators.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)

stories.summary: Water is systematically underpriced yet essential to chip fabrication, agriculture, and power. Droughts in key hubs (Taiwan's fabs, major farming regions) and transboundary disputes can disrupt supply chains in ways markets rarely price in advance.

stories.name: Food & fertilizer fragility

indicators.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)

stories.summary: Food security depends on concentrated fertilizer supply chains - potash, phosphate, and gas-derived nitrogen - exposed to export curbs and shipping chokepoints. Curbs or input shocks feed through to prices with a lag.

stories.name: The lithography (ASML) chokepoint

indicators.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)

stories.summary: ASML is the sole maker of EUV lithography machines, with Zeiss the sole optics supplier - a single point of failure for advanced chips. Export controls, retaliation, or any disruption to this chain would ripple across the entire tech economy.

stories.name: Sea lanes & undersea cables

indicators.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)

stories.summary: A few maritime chokepoints and undersea cables carry most global trade and data. The 2026 Hormuz/Red Sea disruptions showed how quickly closures spike energy and shipping costs; cable cuts are a parallel, under-watched vulnerability.

stories.name: Clearinghouses (financial chokepoint)

indicators.label: Equity volatility (VIX)
Treasury volatility (MOVE)
Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Central counterparties now concentrate the system's risk by design, and Treasury central-clearing mandates will route more activity through them. In a volatility spike, procyclical margin calls can amplify stress - they are a systemic single point of failure.

items.label: 30-year JGB yield (%)
items.rationale: The rubric requires reporting the latest 30-year JGB yield as a percent; 3.88% (June 24, 2026) is the most recent dated print from a preferred primary source and is corroborated within ~2bp by two independent data providers. Confidence is medium rather than high because the last confirmed dated print is June 24, not June 30, and live intraday data from investing.com was not retrievable in structured form.
items.fact: Japan 30-year JGB yield rose to 3.88% on June 24, 2026, per TradingEconomics (tradingeconomics.com/japan/30-year-bond-yield), corroborated by TradingView/LSEG at 3.868% and MacroMicro at 3.86% as of June 3, 2026.

dissent.view: TradingView/LSEG quotes 3.868% as current yield, slightly below TradingEconomics 3.88%
MacroMicro shows 3.86% as of 2026-06-03, an earlier data point

items.label: Direct-lending default rate (%)
items.rationale: Proskauer's index is the most granular, loan-level, primary-source dataset for US direct lending defaults and is the standard benchmark cited by the Fed, Forbes, and the broader leveraged-finance community; 2.73% is the single most recent authoritative print. The rising trend (1.76% → 1.84% → 2.46% → 2.73% over four consecutive quarters) is corroborated by higher PIK income at BDCs and BDC equity discounts, consistent with mounting but not yet systemic funding stress. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (2.73 vs 0.0); held for review]
items.fact: Proskauer's Private Credit Default Index, tracking 697 senior-secured and unitranche US loans totalling $189.2 billion in original principal, recorded a default rate of 2.73% for Q1 2026 — up from 2.46% in Q4 2025 and 1.84% in Q3 2025. Released April 27 2026. Source: https://www.proskauer.com/report/proskauers-private-credit-default-index-reveals-rate-of-273-for-q1-2026

dissent.view: Fitch Ratings reported the US private credit default rate hit a record 6.0% in April 2026 (broader/adjusted definition including distressed exchanges), per Forbes May 25 2026
With Intelligence estimates the 'true' default rate including selective defaults and liability management exercises approaches 5%, versus the sub-2% headline figure
CAIA/Morgan Stanley cite direct lending defaults 'currently running at around 5.6%' (Apr 2026), with Morgan Stanley forecasting a rise to 8%
FS KKR Capital (individual-investor BDC) reported its own portfolio default rate jumped to 8.1% in Q1 2026 from 5.5% in Dec 2025 — a fund-specific reading, not market-wide

items.label: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
items.rationale: All six agencies' rules are still in proposed/comment stage with 18 days to the July 18 deadline, and the Federal Reserve has not issued a standalone prudential proposed rule at all — a documented inter-agency gap confirmed by multiple primary sources. This maps cleanly to rubric score 1 (GAPS: rules still in proposed/comment stage near the deadline, OR documented inter-agency divergence).
items.fact: As of 2026-06-30, all GENIUS Act rules across OCC, FDIC, NCUA, Treasury, FinCEN, and OFAC remain in proposed/NPRM stage — zero final rules have been issued — with 18 days until the statutory July 18, 2026 deadline. The Federal Reserve, a mandated primary federal payment stablecoin regulator, had not published any standalone proposed rule as of June 9, 2026 (only co-signed a joint CIP NPRM on June 18/22 with comment deadline August 21, 2026 — after the finalization deadline). Sources: OCC NPRM Federal Register (2026-03-02, OCC-2025-0372); FDIC NPRM Federal Register (2026-04-10, RIN 3064-AG19); FinCEN/OFAC joint NPRM Federal Register (2026-04-10); CIP joint NPRM Sullivan & Cromwell (2026-06-22); American Prospect (2026-06-24); Chapman & Cutler GENIUS Act Rulemaking Tracker.

dissent.view: Paradigm's rulemaking tracker notes that implementation timelines are 'largely suggestive' and agencies face no formal penalties for missing the deadline, implying the gap may be procedurally tolerated rather than constituting a hard failure — softening the severity of the gap reading.

The American Prospect (2026-06-24) characterizes the Federal Reserve's absence from solo rulemaking as creating a risk that OCC/FDIC will finalize rules without Fed alignment, which some analysts read as trending toward a score-2 (deadline miss) outcome rather than a score-1 gap.

items.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: Six or more named large funds — including the biggest non-traded BDCs by AUM (BCRED, ASIF, Apollo Debt Solutions, Cliffwater CCLFX) — have enforced 5% quarterly repurchase caps with substantial proration in both Q1 and Q2 2026, far exceeding the rubric threshold of >=3 largest funds or combined AUM >$100B; this maps unambiguously to level 2 (WIDESPREAD).
items.fact: In Q2 2026, at least five named large private-credit/BDC funds are enforcing their 5% quarterly repurchase caps after requests far exceeded them: Apollo Debt Solutions BDC (~16.8% requested, 5% honored, ~$2.4B demand), Ares Strategic Income Fund (~14.4% requested, 5% honored), Blackstone Private Credit Fund/BCRED (~10% requested, 5% honored), Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund (~17% requested, 5% honored), and BlackRock Private Credit Fund/BDEBT (~5.3% requested, 5% honored); Morgan Stanley, HPS, and Monroe also capped at 5%. In Q1 2026 the same pattern held for ASIF, Apollo, Cliffwater, BlackRock, JPMorgan, and Morgan Stanley. Combined AUM of BCRED + ASIF + Apollo + Cliffwater alone exceeds $100B. Sources: SEC filings (BCRED SC TO-I/A June 4 2026; BDEBT 8-K 2026), AltsWire/PitchBook (June 25-26 2026), Congress.gov CRS IN12674 (April 2 2026), Stanger (May 14 2026).

dissent.view: Stanger (May 14 2026) notes that despite proration, no non-traded BDC has 'unmet' redemption requests and that caps are functioning as contractually designed — characterizing this as structural operation rather than a crisis gate

CAIA/Fitch (April 2026) argue average BDC debt-to-equity of 0.71x and asset coverage cushions of 38.6% mean balance sheets are not in distress; treating cap enforcement as equivalent to a 'gate' conflates structural mechanics with credit stress

items.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
items.rationale: USDT and USDC — the two largest stablecoins (~$190B and ~$78B market cap) — show a maximum peg deviation of ~0.15% (USDT at $0.9985), well within normal arbitrage noise; no corroborating funding-market stress signal exists. MIM (Abracadabra), a smaller crypto-collateralised stablecoin, depegged severely to $0.8065 on June 8 and further to ~$0.29 by June 29 per CoinMarketCap/MetaMask, but with only ~$16M market cap it does not qualify as a 'major' stablecoin under the rubric.
items.fact: USDT quoted at $0.9985 and USDC at $0.9997 per bitcoinfoundation.org (c. week of June 23, 2026); OpenChainBench bench #015 shows USDC/USDT p99 24h spread of 0.14% on Binance (largest of the major pair), confirming USDT is the wider leg vs USD. No SOFR-EFFR spike, MOVE elevation, or repo-market stress signal corroborates any funding-driven depeg for either top-two stablecoin.

crosscheck.error: could not parse or salvage agent JSON

items.label: SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)
items.rationale: The rubric scores 1 ('SLIPPING') when 'an official extension [has been] granted, OR exemptive-relief requests pending.' One formal one-year extension was granted (Feb 2025), and two exemptive-relief requests remain pending and unresolved as of Jun 30, 2026 - both prongs of the rubric-1 trigger are satisfied. No second extension has been announced, so rubric-2 is not met.
items.fact: On Feb 25, 2025, the SEC formally extended the original compliance dates by one year to Dec 31, 2026 (cash) and Jun 30, 2027 (repo) via Exchange Act Release No. 34-102487 (SEC.gov press release, Feb 25, 2025). As of Apr 20, 2026, Commissioner Uyeda confirmed the current dates stand, but the SEC simultaneously published two pending exemptive-relief requests (SIFMA inter-affiliate relief and IIB extraterritorial relief, both published Apr 17, 2026, comment deadline May 29, 2026) that are unresolved as of today. Sep 30, 2025 Uyeda statement noted 'the Commission does not intend to consider further extensions.' No second extension order has been announced.

dissent.view: Commissioner Uyeda stated Sep 30, 2025 that 'the Commission does not intend to consider further extensions,' suggesting the SEC views the current dates as firm and the trajectory as on-track relative to the extended schedule.

items.label: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
items.rationale: The funding path for the supplementary budget was only specified in late May and promises no extra issuance, but the 10Y yield has risen >50bps cumulatively on fiscal-expansion concerns and sits at 27-year highs, with long-end demand visibly softened (April 30Y tail-widening). Auctions are still clearing above the <2 bid-to-cover disorderly threshold and no emergency BOJ operation is evidenced, placing this in UNCLEAR (1): yield significantly elevated on fiscal concern, funding path now specified but credibility still questioned by markets.
items.fact: Japan 10-year JGB yield at ~2.65% on 2026-06-30 (TradingEconomics/Reuters), up from ~2.10% at start of 2026 — a cumulative >50bps rise over the Takaichi fiscal-expansion period — driven by BOJ normalization, yen weakness, Iran-war energy costs, and fiscal concerns. The May 25 supplementary budget (~¥3 trillion) promised no net new bond issuance (CNBC 2026-05-25). MOF auction results (June 2 10Y, June 23 5Y, June 25 20Y) show auctions clearing with no bid-to-cover below 2 and no BOJ emergency operation reported.

dissent.view: Some analysts (IG International, Feb 2026) note that much of the yield rise has 'since reversed as upcoming expenditure has been largely priced in' and domestic-buyer buffers remain intact, suggesting orderly adjustment closer to 0.

items.label: Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
items.rationale: Multiple broad-universe aggregators (CoinDesk, MacroMicro, DefiLlama-sourced) converge on ~$320-322B as of late May 2026 ATH; applying the prevailing ~$1-2B/week net issuance pace through end-June yields ~$325B, mapping directly to the rubric unit of total USD stablecoin supply in billions.
items.fact: CoinDesk reported total stablecoin market cap at a record $322B on May 26, 2026 (source: coindesk.com, May 26 2026); MacroMicro logged $321.21B on May 24, 2026; StablecoinBeat (top-12 tracker) shows $307.5B by Jun 2026; Motley Fool (June 2026) implies ~$289B on a narrower universe with USDT at $188B (65% share). Extrapolating modest continued growth through June 30 yields an estimated ~$325B comprehensive total.

dissent.view: StablecoinBeat (top-12 coins only) reports $307.5B for June 2026 — a systematic undercount vs. full-universe trackers
Newhedge reports $265.57B (recent, ~mid-June 2026) — likely tracks a narrower PAX/USDP-anchored subset and materially understates total supply
Motley Fool (June 2026) implies ~$289B based on USDT $188B at 65% share — narrower coin universe than DefiLlama/CoinDesk aggregate

items.label: Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)
items.rationale: A documentation backlog and two pending, unresolved exemptive-relief requests are explicitly flagged six months before the Dec 31 2026 cash deadline, and the SIFMA readiness survey shows 'very confident' confidence below 60% for cash and well below 60% for repo — matching the rubric's FRICTION criteria precisely. No deadline extension or operational failure has been announced, so level 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: TBAC Q2 2026 presentation (home.treasury.gov, May 29 2026) states: 'Largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation associated with the bilateral agreements between participants' and 'Legal and documentation issues remain a challenge for the industry'; the SEC published two pending exemptive-relief requests (SIFMA inter-affiliate, IIB extraterritorial) with a May 29 2026 comment deadline, both still unresolved at the TBAC presentation date; the Nov 2025 SIFMA/DTCC/BNY/Broadridge pulse survey (n=330) found only 54% of firms 'very confident' they will be ready for the Dec 31 2026 cash deadline.

dissent.view: ICI filed a comment letter requesting a minimum 24-month extension (to at least June 30 2028 for repo), characterising remaining issues as too complex to resolve in time — a more pessimistic read than the SEC/TBAC consensus that deadlines stand.

SEC Commissioner Uyeda (Sep 30 2025) stated 'the Commission does not intend to consider further extensions' and encouraged firms to target the current deadlines, implying official confidence that on-time compliance is achievable.

items.label: BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: 30+ DPD is up 29 bps YoY — rising but well below the 100 bps threshold for level 2. Charge-offs (~1.83% per CFPB 2023 data; ~3.5% cumulative cohort GMV per Affirm) remain contained below the 5% spike threshold and the 3% containment ceiling, firmly placing this in rubric level 1.
items.fact: Affirm FQ3'26 SEC filing (affirmfq326shareholderle.htm, filed ~May 2026): 30+ DPD on monthly installment loans ex-Peloton/Pay-in-X rose 29 bps YoY to 2.8% (vs. 2.5% in FQ3'25); allowance for credit losses at 6.0% of loans held for investment; cumulative net charge-offs on recent cohorts tracking ~3.5% of cohort GMV, in-line with expectations.

dissent.view: Richmond Fed (Feb 2026 EB-26-05) characterises BNPL financial-stability impact as 'limited at present' with 'no clear evidence of elevated stress,' suggesting a more benign reading closer to level 0 on systemic grounds.

CFPB Dec 2025 market spotlight found charge-off rates and late-fee frequency declined through 2023, which if extrapolated would support level 0; however Affirm's own 2026 SEC filings show delinquency has since risen YoY.

items.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
items.rationale: The last explicitly cited office-sector CMBS delinquency rate from Trepp is 11.20% for February 2026; the May 2026 overall report notes three property types moved lower but does not publish a specific office rate in available excerpts, so 11.20% remains the most recent hard figure. At ~11%, office CMBS delinquency is sharply elevated versus historical norms and near all-time highs, reflecting deep structural stress rather than idiosyncratic single-loan events.
items.fact: Trepp CMBS office delinquency rate fell 114 basis points to 11.20 percent in February 2026, pulling back from January's all-time high of 12.34 percent, driven by modifications and extensions of five large matured office loans. Source: multihousingnews.com / Trepp, reported ~June 2026.

dissent.view: January 2026 office CMBS delinquency was 12.34%, an all-time high per Trepp — 114 bps above the February reading used as the primary value.

items.label: Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)
items.rationale: YTD insured losses of $20 billion are below — not above — the 10-year average, and no individual event breached the $10 billion threshold, satisfying every condition of the NORMAL (0) tier. The 2026 Atlantic hurricane season (peak: Aug–Oct) had not yet produced any named-storm insured losses as of the report date.
items.fact: Global insured catastrophe losses in Q1 2026 totaled $20 billion — 26% below the 10-year Q1 decadal average of $26 billion — with no single event exceeding $10 billion insured; Aon independently confirms ~$20 billion. Source: Gallagher Re Q1 2026 Natural Catastrophe and Climate Report (ajg.com/gallagherre, April 21 2026) and Aon Q1 2026 Global Catastrophe Recap.

dissent.view: Aon characterizes the same $20 billion Q1 figure as '6% above the 21st-century average,' implying a slightly above-average reading on a longer historical baseline rather than the 10-year window used in the rubric.

items.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
items.rationale: The state count of active non-renewal/exit pressure (approximately 13 states identified across wildfire, Gulf-coast, and severe-convective-storm corridors) squarely places this in the 5-15 state RISING band; however, AM Best revised the segment outlook to Stable in Dec 2025, reinsurance costs are falling, and no top-10 insurer has fully exited a major market, all of which preclude the ACCELERATING (2) classification.
items.fact: A consumer/industry roundup published June 2026 identifies 13 states experiencing active non-renewal waves in 2026 (California, Florida, Texas, Louisiana, Colorado, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Nebraska, Minnesota, Georgia, and others); AM Best revised the US homeowners segment outlook from Negative to Stable in December 2025; NAIC issued a nationwide homeowners market data call (deadline extended to July 15, 2026) specifically to monitor non-renewals, cancellations, and coverage access across all 50 states (NAIC, March 26, 2026; Insurance Journal, April 1, 2026). No top-10 insurer has fully exited a major state: State Farm remains closed to NEW business in California but has not fully exited, and Florida market is stabilizing with Citizens receiving an 8.7% rate decrease (AM Best / HousingWire, mid-2026).

dissent.view: Climatecosmos.com (June 2026) frames the same 13-state list as an 'accelerating' trend and notes the non-renewal pace has not reversed since the Treasury reported a 30% surge through 2022; some analysts characterize this as structural, not cyclical.

Insurance.com / AM Best (Dec 2025) characterize the market as 'improving' and 'stable', implying the non-renewal wave is peaking rather than accelerating, which could argue for a STABLE (0) reading in aggregate national terms.

items.label: Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Two FDIC failures in the window map to rubric level 1 (ISOLATED: 1-2 firm-specific failures). CRE linkage on Metropolitan is partial/indirect (real estate loan tied to a Ponzi fraud) and Community Bank's formal supervisory cause was governance/compliance; neither was labelled systemic CRE stress by regulators, and no supervisory body issued a systemic regional-bank CRE warning. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: FDIC failed-bank list shows exactly 2 bank failures in the trailing 6 months: Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust (Chicago, IL, failed Jan 30 2026, $261M assets) and Community Bank and Trust - West Georgia (LaGrange, GA, failed May 1 2026, $288M assets). Bisnow (Feb 2026) reported Metropolitan had CRE/real estate loan exposure tied to a fraud case; Community Bank received a Fed enforcement action in April 2026 citing governance/capital deficiencies. No supervisory agency issued a systemic CRE-regional bank warning. Source: fdic.gov/bank-failures/failed-bank-list (updated May 1 2026); fdic.gov press release May 1 2026; Bisnow Feb 2 2026.

dissent.view: vaasblock.com (Jun 2026) characterises 2025-2026 regional bank CRE stress as broader earnings-and-capital pressure across multiple institutions with geographic/sector CRE concentrations, suggesting stress exceeds two isolated cases even if formal failures are limited to two.

items.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
items.rationale: The February 2026 Fitch figure of 6.80% is the most recent hard data point available from the primary rater of subprime auto ABS; it maps directly to the rubric's requested latest subprime auto 60+ day delinquency rate.
items.fact: Per Fitch Ratings subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency index, the rate edged down to 6.80% in February 2026, after hitting a record 6.90% in January 2026 (up 34 bps YoY); March data was not yet released as of the May 19, 2026 Wolf Street report citing Fitch.

dissent.view: S&P Global Ratings reported the full-year 2025 subprime auto 60+ day delinquency average at a record 6.18% (vs. 5.78% in 2024), a different metric (annual average vs. monthly point-in-time).

items.label: Large public-pension funded ratio (%)
items.rationale: The Milliman PPFI is the preferred primary source per the rubric and its April 30, 2026 reading of 87.6% is the most recent published monthly estimate for large US public pensions; the rubric asks for a representative funded ratio as a percent, which maps directly to 87.6.
items.fact: Milliman 100 Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): 'The PPFI funded ratio stood at 87.6% as of April 30' (Milliman, public-pension-funding-index-may-2026, released June 3, 2026), covering the 100 largest US public DB plans with $6.009T in assets vs. $6.860T in total pension liability.

dissent.view: Equable Institute (State of Pensions 2025, Jan 8 2026) reports 82.5% aggregate funded ratio for US state and local retirement systems as of December 31, 2025 — a broader universe and an older valuation date, but a formal annual study figure.

items.label: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: The window contains 1-2 new or materially restructured IMF arrangements (Ukraine new EFF, Pakistan augmented EFF) but zero executed missed coupons or SD ratings, placing the indicator in the RISING band (1-2 new IMF arrangements, no missed coupon). Insufficient evidence of a >=3-sovereign cluster to reach CLUSTER (2).
items.fact: No confirmed new sovereign missed coupons or SD ratings found in Jan-Jun 2026. Ukraine received a new EFF in early 2026 (IMF Country Report 26/58, imf.org) and Pakistan received a ~$1.2B disbursement approval under its existing $7B EFF on ~28 Mar 2026 (Bloomberg, 2026-03-28). Zambia concluded its ECF in Jan 2026 and is negotiating a successor (IMF press briefing, 2026-06-25). No >=3 new distress-driven arrangements and zero missed coupons confirmed.

dissent.view: Ukraine's new EFF could be categorised as a continuation/successor rather than a fresh distress-driven program, which would reduce the count toward CALM (0)
Senegal is in active IMF discussions about potential debt restructuring as of Jun 2026, which if executed could push toward CLUSTER (2)

items.label: Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
items.rationale: Both the primary regulatory sources (ESMA June 2026 and TPR 2025) characterise LDI buffers as adequate and operations as normal, with no regulator flagging an active liquidity stress episode or margin-call event in 2025-2026; this maps squarely to rubric level 0 (NONE). The residual ESMA caveat that 'a subset of funds might still face challenges' under a large shock is a forward-looking stress-scenario observation, not an observed episode, and does not meet the threshold for level 1.
items.fact: ESMA's 23 June 2026 TRV article (ESMA50-1949966494-4208) finds that LDI fund resilience has materially improved since 2022: the stress-test liquidity shortfall declined from EUR 9bn to EUR 3bn by end-2024, driven by reduced leverage and a smaller segment (NAV EUR 113bn vs EUR 130bn at crisis). TPR's own market-oversight report (published 2025, data to 31 March 2025) confirms rate buffers doubled to ~300 bps, LDI market halved to ~GBP 0.7trn, and that 'interest rate volatility has returned at various moments in 2025 and the experience has been that LDI related operations have been normal.' No margin-call episode, no forced gilt selling, and no central-bank intervention has been reported in 2025-2026. Sources: ESMA (23 Jun 2026), TPR market-oversight report (2025).

items.label: Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)
items.rationale: No new sovereign reserve seizure/freeze qualifies for band 2; the original Russian freeze predates 2026 and no confiscation of principal has been executed. BRICS Pay expansion and the 'Unit' gold-backed token are active rhetoric and pilot-stage infrastructure, meeting band 1 (new non-dollar settlement launches/rhetoric without operational major-economy reserve shift).
items.fact: As of June 2026, no new sovereign reserve freeze or outright confiscation has been executed beyond the original 2022 G7/EU freeze of ~$280bn in Russian CBR assets (still frozen, not seized). On non-dollar settlement, BRICS Pay is described as entering an 'operational phase' in 2026 with pilot links between national systems (CIPS, UPI, SPFS, Pix), but authoritative sources (EBC Financial Group, 13 Apr 2026; bricspayment.ru citing Russian FM Lavrov at Rio summit) confirm full deployment is targeted for the New Delhi BRICS summit (late 2026) and a working prototype is not expected until 2027-2028; no major economy has operationally moved reserve settlement off USD. Sources: Brookings Jun 2026, CFR Jun 2026, IAI Apr 2026, EBC Apr 2026, bricspayment.ru.

dissent.view: Some secondary sources (informedclearly.com, watcher.guru) claim BRICS Pay is already 'operationally launched' in 2026 and has reduced USD intra-bloc trade by two-thirds, which if true could approach band 2 threshold for a major economy moving off USD settlement
Wikipedia (May 2026) notes Moscow Arbitration Court granted Russia's $229bn claim against Euroclear on 15 May 2026, a legal counter-move, not a Western seizure

items.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
items.rationale: The 2026 trustees report worsens the OASI depletion date by one quarter (vs. 2025 report) and the 75-year actuarial deficit grew 16% to 4.42% of taxable payroll, satisfying rubric band 1 (trustees report worsens depletion date or reform actively debated). No automatic cut has been triggered (depletion is projected for 2032, not yet reached) and no enacted cuts exist, so band 2 is not met.
items.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026, ssa.gov/oact/trsum/) projects OASI trust fund depletion in Q4 2032 -- one quarter earlier than the 2025 report -- with only 78% of scheduled benefits payable at that date; the combined OASDI depletion remains 2034 (unchanged). No automatic benefit cut has been triggered and no reform legislation has been enacted; the Bipartisan Social Security Commission Act of 2026 (H.R. 9187) was introduced but not passed.

dissent.view: AARP/SSA press release emphasises combined OASDI depletion date of 2034 -- unchanged from 2025 -- framing the outlook as stable, while Bipartisan Policy Center and PGPF stress the OASI-only date of 2032 as a one-year acceleration; the two framings reflect the same underlying data but different fund combinations.

items.label: Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
items.rationale: Rubric level 2 (RETALIATION) is met: China enacted affirmative countermeasures—entity-list additions explicitly framed as retaliation plus new statutory retaliation authority—within the trailing 3-month window, alongside concurrent US/allied TIGHTENING actions; volume signal (ASML China share 36%→19%) anchors the disruption read.
items.fact: Within the trailing 3 months (Apr–Jun 2026), both sides enacted documented measures. US/allied: (1) MATCH Act introduced ~Apr 2, 2026 proposing blanket DUV ban on ASML/Japan tools to China (CNBC, Apr 7 2026); (2) BIS guidance ~Jun 1, 2026 affirming Blackwell chip restrictions apply to overseas subsidiaries of China-HQ companies (Al Jazeera, Jun 1 2026). China retaliation: (3) MOFCOM Jun 22, 2026 added 10 US firms—including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth—to its export control list, explicitly as retaliation for Pentagon military-company designations (Al Jazeera/Yahoo Finance, Jun 22 2026); (4) China enacted Jun 11, 2026 tech-transfer regulations granting statutory retaliation authority against nations restricting Chinese tech investments (abhs.in citing FT, Jun 2026); (5) MOFCOM Announcement No. 26, Jun 24, 2026, formalised a whistleblower/reporting mechanism for strategic-mineral dual-use export-control violations, effective Jul 1 2026 (geopolitechs.org, Jun 2026). ASML China revenue share fell from 36% (Q4 2025) to 19% (Q1 2026), corroborating demand compression from cumulative controls.

dissent.view: East Asia Forum (Mar 11 2026) characterises the Trump White House as 'cooling' chip controls and prioritising trade-talk stability, suggesting the executive branch posture is more TIGHTENING than full RETALIATION escalation; congressional proposals (MATCH Act) remain unenacted.

items.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
items.rationale: A signed MOU, active diplomatic talks, partial transits via northern/Omani routes, and Brent at ~$74 all satisfy the rubric's CONTESTED definition (threatened, partially disrupted, active de-escalation/MOU in effect). The rubric explicitly caps the rating at 1 when convoys are moving and a signed truce is in place, regardless of reduced volumes; the benign oil price also precludes a rating of 2.
items.fact: US-Iran MOU signed 17 June 2026 established 60-day ceasefire and strait reopening; Iran reinstated PGSA toll requirements on 21 June but 25 AIS-visible transits recorded 22 June including LNG carriers; JMIC widened Omani southern route on 27 June; Intertanko confirmed northern and southern routes 'fully open'; VP Vance confirmed 16M bbl transited 21 June (record); Brent crude ~$74/bbl as of 29-30 June; US-Iran Doha talks resuming 30 June per straits.live. Sources: IMO (imo.org, 26 Jun), CFR (cfr.org, 17 Jun), HSToday (21-22 Jun), Wikipedia 2026 Hormuz crisis (updated 30 Jun), CBS News (29 Jun), straits.live (30 Jun).

dissent.view: straits.live labels the strait 'effectively closed' (Day 121) citing only 5 ships/day vs ~93 normal, characterising it as a commercial closure despite the MOU and talks
hormuztracking.com reported zero outbound commercial transits as of 23 June and described an 'unprecedented operational standstill'

items.label: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)
items.rationale: Four or more new major-economy measures against Chinese exports were initiated or took effect within the trailing 3 months, satisfying the rubric threshold of >=4 new measures for ESCALATING=2; the USTR June 2 12.5% Section 301 proposal and EU steel quota doubling (>=100% increase in out-of-quota duties) also independently qualify as broad sectoral tariff rounds, even though the US-China bilateral truce has not formally collapsed.
items.fact: In the trailing 3 months (Apr-Jun 2026), at least 4 distinct major-economy trade measures targeting Chinese exports were initiated or finalised: (1) USTR June 2 2026 proposed 12.5% Section 301 tariff on China (forced-labor investigation, China-Briefing/USTR, June 3 2026); (2) USTR March 11 2026 Section 301 investigation into structural excess capacity covering China (Tax Foundation tracker, May 2026); (3) USTR March 12 2026 Section 301 forced-labor investigation covering China among 60 countries (Trade Compliance Resource Hub, June 3 2026); (4) EU steel safeguard reform effective July 1 2026 cutting tariff-free quotas 47% and doubling out-of-quota duties from 25% to 50% (Atlantic Council, June 3 2026); plus EU ceramic tableware AD duty raised to 79% on Feb 6 2026 and EU parquet flooring absorption investigation ongoing. US-China bilateral truce (extended to Nov 10 2026) remains nominally intact but USTR Section 301 proposals represent explicit escalation outside truce scope.

items.label: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
items.rationale: The truce has not formally lapsed (ruling out score 2), but CSIS price and volume data confirm a partial breach on gallium — exports effectively ceased and prices are at record highs despite the suspension — plus the April 2025 heavy-REE licensing controls were never covered by the truce, collectively satisfying the rubric's 'partial breach' condition for score 1.
items.fact: MOFCOM Announcements 55-58/61/62 (Nov 7, 2025) suspended China's Oct-2025 rare-earth export controls through Nov 10, 2026 — nominal expiry is >90 days away. However, CSIS (May 22, 2026, csis.org) documents a partial breach: gallium exports from China remain near-zero, with spot prices hitting a record $1,850/kg in April 2026 (+200% YoY), and China's April 2025 Announcement No. 18 controls on seven heavy REEs were never suspended and remain fully active. MOFCOM also issued Announcement No. 26 (June 24, 2026) tightening enforcement of strategic-mineral export controls from July 1, 2026.

dissent.view: White House and MOFCOM formally treat the Oct-2025 controls as suspended through Nov 10, 2026, and describe the truce as holding; no formal reimposition has been announced.

items.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
items.rationale: Licenses are being issued but only through a non-automatic, client-specific whitelist regime (three major exporters with partial customer coverage), reviews exceed normal timelines, and HREE-bearing grades require individual end-use documentation — squarely matching rubric level 1 (SLOWING). The June 2026 entity-list action adds further selective denial for named US firms but does not constitute a categorical halt across all applicants, so level 2 is not warranted. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: As of late June 2026, China's April 2025 MOFCOM Announcement No. 18 controls on seven HREEs (Sm, Gd, Tb, Dy, Lu, Sc, Y) and HREE-bearing magnets remain fully in force, requiring per-shipment or 'general' export licenses. Only three named producers (JL MAG, Ningbo Yunsheng, Zhong Ke San Huan) hold 'general licences' covering a subset of their customer lists (Reuters/Bloomberg, Dec 2025; Mainrich June 2026). Standard N-grade NdFeB without Dy/Tb exports without friction; H/SH/UH/EH grades and SmCo require a license with end-use documentation for every shipment. Separately, on 22 June 2026 MOFCOM Announcement No. 23 added 10 US entities (including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth) to the export control entity list, imposing presumptive denial on them (SMM/metal.com, ~22 Jun 2026). The Oct 2025 expanded extraterritorial controls remain suspended until 10 Nov 2026 (MOFCOM Announcement No. 70/2025).

dissent.view: White House framed Dec 2025 general-licence issuance as 'de facto end' of export controls, implying near-normal flow (level 0); Chinese officials did not confirm broader easing and shipments year-to-date remained ~20% below prior year.

items.label: Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)
items.rationale: Zero operational ≤3nm capacity exists outside Taiwan as of June 2026 - Arizona is at 4nm and Japan is at 12-28nm; overseas ≤3nm fabs are under construction with earliest production in 2H 2027 (Arizona) and 2028 (Japan), placing this squarely in the EXTREME (<10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab) category.
items.fact: As of Q2 2026, all TSMC leading-edge (<=3nm and 2nm) production capacity is located exclusively in Taiwan (Fab 20 Hsinchu and Fab 22 Kaohsiung, ~180,000-200,000 wafers/month at 3nm by end-2026; 2nm ramping to ~100,000 WPM). TSMC Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 operates at 4nm only; Phase 2 (3nm) begins tool installation Q3 2026 with mass production targeted 2H 2027. JASM Kumamoto Fab 2 (3nm-upgraded) targets equipment install and production in 2028. Taiwan Ministry of Economic Affairs estimates sub-5nm production remains ~85% Taiwan-based through 2030. Sources: TSMC 2025 Annual Report (investor.tsmc.com); TSMC 6-K Q1 2026 (SEC, April 2026); TrendForce April 27 2026; Taiwan Ministry of Economic Affairs via TrendForce Feb 6 2026; Taipei Times April 1 2026.

dissent.view: Analysts project overseas capacity (all nodes) hitting ~20% of TSMC total by 2028, and sub-2nm mix could reach 30% US / 70% Taiwan by 2030, suggesting trajectory toward rubric level 1 within 4 years - but these are forward projections, not current operational reality.

items.label: Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)
items.rationale: Rubric level 1 (THREATENED) applies: multiple credible, active threat vectors exist — shadow fleet in Baltic, Iran conflict risk at Hormuz, residual Houthi activity near Bab el-Mandeb — but no primary source confirms a major cable currently severed and unrepaired as of 2026-06-30, so level 2 (CUT) is not warranted.
items.fact: No confirmed currently-severed and unrepaired major submarine cable identified in Red Sea, Hormuz, or Baltic as of late June 2026. The most recent confirmed mass-cut event was ~6 Sep 2025 (Bab el-Mandeb: SEA-ME-WE-4, IMEWE, EIG, FALCON/GCX, ~15 cables total per Marine Technology News, Dec 2025), which has since been rerouted/repaired. TeleGeography (May 4, 2026) and AGBI (Mar 2026) assess Hormuz cables as at elevated risk but intact. Forbes (Jun 26, 2026) flags ongoing Baltic shadow-fleet threat but names no new 2026 cut. AUKUS communique (May 31, 2026) cites 'unprecedented' pace of cuts over 18 months without naming a current active break.

dissent.view: Australian Defence Minister Marles (AUKUS, May 2026) stated cables are being cut 'at an unprecedented rate' over the past 18 months, implying ongoing active incidents, though no specific currently-unrepaired cable is named.

Better World Campaign (Mar 2026) states '2024 and 2025 disruptions rippled across Middle East and South Asia after cables were severed by Iranian-backed Houthi forces', and Meta paused 2Africa Pearls in the Gulf — suggesting continued active disruption environment.

items.label: AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)
items.rationale: The DOE 202(c) emergency order authorizing PJM curtailment of data-center-class load, combined with actual demand-response activation across Mid-Atlantic zones on May 18-19 2026, constitutes a confirmed grid-emergency curtailment of data-center load — the rubric-2 threshold. The Seattle power-scarcity-driven moratorium (June 9 2026) is an additional rubric-2 indicator; together these push the reading firmly to ACUTE.
items.fact: On May 18, 2026, the U.S. DOE issued a Section 202(c) emergency order authorizing PJM Interconnection to curtail data centers and other large loads with backup generation; PJM simultaneously activated pre-emergency demand response in the Dominion, BGE, and PEPCO areas due to <5,800 MW reserve margins during a heat/maintenance crunch (Utility Dive, May 19 2026). Separately, on June 9 2026 Seattle City Council enacted an emergency moratorium on new data center siting citing grid capacity constraints (Seattle City Council, June 9 2026). LBL 'Queued Up' (May 2026 update, emp.lbl.gov) reports 2,060+ GW in U.S. interconnection queues as of end-2025, with median queue duration doubled to >4 years. IEA Electricity 2026 (iea.org, ~June 2026) confirms 2,500+ GW stalled in global queues and 'many power systems already experiencing rising congestion-related curtailment.'

dissent.view: IEA April 2026 'Key Questions on Energy and AI' frames the dominant dynamic as developers bypassing slow queues via onsite gas generation rather than forced curtailment of operating facilities — consistent with TIGHT (1) rather than ACUTE (2) as a systemic characterization

LBL Queued Up 2025 edition notes a 12% decrease in total active queue volume year-over-year through end-2024, suggesting queues may be beginning to self-correct via withdrawals, which would point toward TIGHT rather than ACUTE

items.label: China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
items.rationale: The mid-March 2026 addition of NK-blend and further phosphate bans on top of existing urea quotas and the Dec-2025 NDRC phosphate suspension constitutes a NEW/BROADENED restriction within the trailing 3 months, satisfying rubric level 2; the May 2026 urea quota reopen is itself a controlled quota (not a lifting), and the phosphate ban remains in place through August 2026.
items.fact: NDRC suspended phosphate fertilizer exports through August 2026 (directed Dec 2025; confirmed by S&P Global Platts, Exiger, SunSirs). In mid-March 2026, Beijing added a NEW ban on nitrogen-potassium (NK) blends and additional phosphate varieties (Reuters/Bloomberg, ~18 Mar 2026). For urea, export quotas were suspended Jan-May 2026 (only 461,500 t shipped Jan-Mar); on ~26 May 2026, NDRC issued a fresh 1.5-1.6 mt quota for Jun-Aug with a mandatory price floor of $660/t FOB prilled and $670/t FOB granular - still a tightly controlled quota regime, not unrestricted exports (Profercy, 26 May 2026; Keyuan Fertilizer, 29 May 2026). Phosphate ban remains intact. Between half and three-quarters of China's fertilizer exports are restricted (Exiger/Reuters, Apr 2026).

dissent.view: The Philippines reported China indicated fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though Chinese officials never publicly confirmed this (Reuters, Mar 2026)

items.label: Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: The rubric's level-2 threshold of '>10 jurisdictions with enacted pauses/cancellations' is clearly met by the 20+ enacted local moratoriums in Michigan alone, plus confirmed enacted pauses in RI, NV, NY towns, CT, MO, and ME localities. A statewide enacted moratorium has not yet been confirmed, but the >10 local enacted threshold independently qualifies as WIDESPREAD.
items.fact: As of mid-June 2026, no statewide moratorium has been signed into law (NY S10642 passed the legislature June 4 but awaits Gov. Hochul's signature; Maine LD 307 was vetoed April 24). However, enacted local construction pauses exceed 10 jurisdictions: Michigan alone has 20+ local moratoriums, plus Smithfield RI (outright ban), Reno NV (May 14 pause), Lysander NY (May 7), Town of Perth NY, Groton CT, Peculiar MO, Bangor ME, Seattle WA (in committee/emergency moratorium cleared May 20), and Indianapolis IN (non-binding pause-resolution June 3), among others. Source: datacenterbans.com (June 1, 2026); Rockefeller Institute of Government (June 2026); MultiState (June 2026).

dissent.view: datacenterbans.com characterized (June 1, 2026) 'the path to broad statewide data center moratoriums remained closed,' implying no statewide enactment — consistent with our reading, but suggesting a more cautious overall assessment of the movement's success.

theaiconsultingnetwork.com stated (April 2026) 'Maine is the only state to pass a statewide data center moratorium into law' — but this is now outdated/inaccurate; Maine's bill was subsequently vetoed April 24, 2026.

items.label: Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)
items.rationale: The latest ICSG forecast (April 2026) places 2026 refined copper balance at +96,000 t surplus, well above the +100kt surplus threshold in the rubric. This unambiguously maps to score 0 (SURPLUS) under the stated rubric.
items.fact: ICSG 'Copper Market Forecast 2026-2027' (April 2026): global refined copper market forecast at a SURPLUS of ~96,000 metric tons in 2026, reversing the prior October 2025 forecast of a 150,000 t deficit; driven by lower-than-expected demand growth (usage now +1.6% vs prior +2.1%) and higher secondary refined production. Source: ICSG.org April 2026 press release, corroborated by Recycling Today (Apr 28 2026) and ADMIS (Apr 24 2026).

dissent.view: J.P. Morgan projects a 330,000 mt refined copper deficit for 2026, driven by hyperscale AI data-center demand
ICSG October 2025 forecast (now superseded) projected a 150,000 t deficit for 2026

items.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
items.rationale: A confirmed government water-rationing order at Taiwan's Hsinchu fab hub satisfies the rubric's ACUTE (2) criterion of a confirmed water-rationing order at a fab region, independent of whether D3/D4 thresholds are met. The US Southwest additionally shows D3 coverage (~30% of NM) corroborating regional severity.
items.fact: Taiwan's Water Resources Agency imposed water rationing after the driest winter in 75 years, explicitly affecting chipmaking hub Hsinchu County; emergency measures authorised through July 2026 (Taipei Times, 2026-03-27, https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/front/archives/2026/03/27/2003854549). TSMC CEO C.C. Wei warned of mounting water-supply constraints in June 2026 (Reuters/Astute Group, 2026-06). Separately, US Southwest (New Mexico) shows D2=55% and D3=30% area coverage per USDM ~2026-06-24 (droughtmonitor.unl.edu/Data/pdf/current/current_NM_cat.pdf).

dissent.view: US Southwest drought has been improving: USDM West page notes snowpack 'returned to near-normal' with D1-D4 improvements in NM/SW Colorado, suggesting conditions may be easing from peak severity

items.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
items.rationale: The LBNL 2025 Edition is the latest published PDF report with a named median/average wait figure; 55 months maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest median grid interconnection wait in months. Confidence is medium rather than high because the 55-month figure is characterized as an 'average' for the 2024 COD cohort rather than a strictly labeled 'median,' and the May 2026 Excel data update through EOY 2025 has not yet been translated into a revised published PDF narrative figure.
items.fact: LBNL 'Queued Up: 2025 Edition' (published December 2025, data through end of 2024): 'The typical project reaching commercial operation in 2024 spent an average of 55 months (4.5 years) in the queue.' Source: emp.lbl.gov/publications/queued-2025-edition-characteristics, corroborated by loopbacksystems.com summary of LBNL 2025 report.

dissent.view: LBNL 2025 Edition states median duration from IR to COD is 'over 4 years' (>48 months) for projects built 2018-2024 broadly, which is slightly lower than the 55-month average for the 2024 COD cohort specifically; the broader cohort figure suggests ~50-54 months.

items.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
items.rationale: The strait is physically open post-June 17 MOU — 42 transits on June 28 and Brent at $74 preclude a rating of 2 (CLOSED). However, fertilizer/sulfur/ammonia flows remain severely below pre-war norms (~6-30% of normal volumes depending on source), war-risk premiums remain elevated, mine-clearing is incomplete, and full normalization is weeks away, confirming the rubric-1 DISRUPTED state.
items.fact: Windward MIOC (June 28, 2026): 42 total Hormuz transits recorded (28 inbound, 14 outbound); 'Strait remains open with no disruption to freedom of navigation.' Fertilizer flows: Kpler/Bloomberg (week ending June 21) show at least 16 fertilizer vessels exited carrying ~530,000 tonnes, nearing pre-war weekly levels for that window — but WTO Trade Tracker records fertilizer cargoes still ~94% below pre-war by early June. Brent crude at $74.06, well below crisis peak of $126, ruling out a corroborating price signal for full closure. War-risk insurance and mine-clearing incomplete. Sources: insights.windward.ai; ttnews.com/articles/hormuz-fertilizer-recover; straits.live; datalab.wto.org/Strait-of-Hormuz-Trade-Tracker.

dissent.view: Straits.live rates the strait 'effectively closed to commercial shipping' as of June 30, reporting only 5 transits vs ~93/day normal, implying a near-total shutdown still in effect.

WTO Trade Tracker reports fertilizer-related cargoes outbound still ~94% below pre-war levels through early June, suggesting the partial reopening had not yet translated into meaningful cargo flows at the time of its last update.

items.label: Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
items.rationale: At 28.5-29% of capacity, Lake Mead breaches the rubric's <30% CRITICAL threshold; simultaneously, at ~1,045 ft the reservoir sits within 10 feet of Hoover Dam's ~1,035-ft power-generation cliff, independently satisfying the 'within 15 feet of a power-generation threshold' CRITICAL criterion. Both conditions are confirmed by USBR physical data, not merely forecast.
items.fact: Lake Mead storage reported at 28.5% of capacity (7.45M AF) as of 2026-06-29 per Colorado River Monitor citing USBR data; USBR weekly report (June 15) placed elevation at 1,047.14 ft, and a June 26 update placed it at ~1,045 ft — approximately 10 feet above Hoover Dam's 1,035-ft power-generation critical threshold. Newsweek (citing USBR, ~June 15) independently reported 29% full with 7,487,010 AF. Source: https://www.usbr.gov/lc/region/g4000/weekly.pdf; https://coloradorivermonitor.com/lake-mead-water-level

dissent.view: Las Vegas Sun (June 7, 2026) cited Lake Mead at 35% capacity when it crossed 1,050 ft — slightly higher than the late-June 28.5-29% readings, consistent with the continued decline since then.

items.label: Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Three confirmed top-20 mines — Grasberg, Kamoa-Kakula, and TFM — each had active, confirmed production outages or sustained cuts in 2026; Grasberg and Kamoa-Kakula remain on materially reduced trajectories through at least H2 2026, and Goldman Sachs / Benchmark Mineral Intelligence forecasts specifically cite a copper market deficit driven by these combined losses, satisfying the rubric's CLUSTER threshold (>=3 major mines disrupted OR lost output large enough for institutional deficit forecasts).
items.fact: As of late June 2026, at least THREE top-20 copper mines carry confirmed, active production cuts: (1) Grasberg (Indonesia, world #2 by output) — Freeport's Q1 2026 10-K (SEC, filed 2026) shows Q1 2026 output of only 95 million lb Cu vs normal ~425 million lb/quarter due to the Sep 2025 mud-rush; full restart now targeting only 65% capacity in H2 2026, with 2026 full-year copper output cut to ~700 million lb vs pre-incident plan of 1.7 billion lb (mining.com, 8 May 2026). (2) Kamoa-Kakula (DRC, top-5 globally) — Ivanhoe/Zijin cut 2026 guidance to 290,000–330,000 t from an initial 420,000 t target following 2025 seismic flooding; guidance further trimmed in May 2026 (Metalnomist, 30 May 2026; Ecofin Agency, 1 Apr 2026). (3) Tenke Fungurume / TFM (DRC, ~519,000 t/yr) — strike began 1 June 2026, disrupted production; workers returned by 5 June 2026 with CMOC confirming 'small' impact (Bloomberg/mining.com, 5-9 June 2026). TFM strike has resolved but Grasberg and Kamoa remain on multi-year reduced-output recovery trajectories. Goldman Sachs estimated ~270,000 t of lost 2026 Grasberg output alone — enough for institutions to forecast a market deficit (economies.com, Oct 2025).

dissent.view: CMOC characterized TFM's June 2026 strike impact as 'small' and operations 'back to normal' by 5 June, so TFM may not count as a sustained active cut by late June 2026; on that reading only Grasberg + Kamoa remain (SOME, value=1)

items.label: Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The IWT is formally suspended (treaty contested/suspended = rubric level 1 threshold met) and official mutual war threats have been exchanged between defence/foreign ministers. No confirmed deliberate full flow cut-off physically carried out in 2026 is evidenced — India's 'stop all water' statement is prospective, not confirmed as executed — so rubric level 2 is not met.
items.fact: India's Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) remains formally suspended ('in abeyance') since April 2025; in June 2026 Indian EAM Jaishankar confirmed it stays suspended 'until Pakistan completely stops cross-border terrorism' and water minister CR Patil said Pakistan would not get 'a single drop of water in coming years'; Pakistan's defence minister Khawaja Asif stated 'the moment we feel our national security is under threat...we will go to war' while simultaneously saying current conditions do not warrant military action; Pakistan has complained three times (2025 x2, May 2026) of 'abnormal, abrupt flow variations on the Chenab'. Sources: CNBC 22 Jun 2026 (https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/22/india-pakistan-indus-waters-treaty-water-dispute-war-risk.html); Wikipedia IWT (updated ~Jun 2026); Britannica IWT (updated Jun 2026).

dissent.view: Wikipedia states India 'stopped the flow of water on the Chenab River from the Baglihar Dam as a short-term punitive action' in April-May 2025 (post-suspension); if that constitutes a 'deliberate flow cut-off carried out', level 2 could apply, but this was described as short-term and not sustained into 2026.

items.label: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
items.rationale: Capex growth (~77-84% YoY) clearly exceeds AI-revenue growth for AWS (+28%) and is comparable-to-lower than Google Cloud (+63%) and Microsoft AI (+123%), placing the aggregate gap firmly in WIDE territory. AI revenue is still accelerating strongly YoY with no confirmed QoQ deceleration in Q1 2026, ruling out score=2; Meta raised capex without proportional revenue proof but did not cut its AI-revenue outlook, so the score=2 trigger is not cleanly met.
items.fact: Q1 2026 earnings (per Financial Times/Statista aggregation, April 29-30 2026): combined hyperscaler capex guidance for 2026 rose to ~$725B (+77% YoY from ~$410B in 2025); individual quarterly capex: Alphabet $35.67B (>+100% YoY), Amazon $44.2B (~+77% YoY), Microsoft $30.88B (+84% YoY), Meta $20B (+54% YoY, full-year guide raised to $125-145B). AI-attributable revenue: AWS +28% YoY ($37.6B), Google Cloud +63% YoY ($20B), Microsoft AI run-rate $37B annualized (+123% YoY). Allianz Research characterizes the capex-vs-revenue divergence at ~46% (Forbes, June 2, 2026). Sequoia Cahn estimates a $600B annual revenue gap, widening in 2026.

items.label: GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)
items.rationale: CoreWeave is a named borrower with a publicly disclosed 2026 GPU-backed refinancing wall and GPU collateral whose rental rates have declined 50-70%, satisfying the rubric-1 criterion of 'a named borrower facing a refinancing wall.' No default, distressed exchange, or failed refinancing has been confirmed, so rubric-2 is not met.
items.fact: CoreWeave (CRWV) carried a ~$4.2B 2026 refinancing wall on GPU-collateralized debt (DDTL 2.0, originated at ~11% avg rate, repayments beginning Jan 2026) against GPU collateral whose rental rates have fallen 50-70%. CoreWeave partially addressed this by closing an $8.5B investment-grade DDTL 4.0 (A3/Moody's, March 31 2026), a $3.1B DDTL 5.0 (Ba2/Moody's, May 18 2026), and $2.75B in 9.75% senior notes (April 2026) partly earmarked for debt repayment -- but no confirmed default or failed refinancing has occurred. Sources: CoreWeave IR (investors.coreweave.com, March 31 and May 18 2026); Quinn Emanuel client alert (quinnemanuel.com, March 16 2026); Level-Headed Investing / Quartz (qz.com, May 8 2026); BIS Annual Economic Report 2026 (June 28 2026).

items.label: Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
items.rationale: The government is governing but relies on Article 49.3 to pass legislation and survives only because Socialists and Republicans refuse to join no-confidence majorities — a textbook Rubric-1 (FRAGILE) configuration. No dissolution or PM resignation has occurred since Lecornu's reappointment in Oct 2025.
items.fact: PM Sébastien Lecornu's minority government is still in office, having survived multiple no-confidence votes (most recently Jan 23, 2026, when LFI's motion drew 269 votes vs. 288 needed, and Feb 2, 2026, when the budget passed after two further failed motions). Lecornu invoked Article 49.3 in Jan 2026 to force through the 2026 budget. The National Assembly remains a hung parliament with no party holding an absolute majority. Source: Euronews Jan 23 2026; Al Jazeera Feb 2 2026; Wikipedia (Sébastien Lecornu, updated ~Jun 2026).

items.label: Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)
items.rationale: Two of the three major agencies (Fitch and S&P) executed actual one-notch downgrades within the trailing 12 months ending 2026-06-30, satisfying rubric level 2 outright. Moody's negative outlook also independently satisfies level 1, but the confirmed cuts from Fitch and S&P make level 2 the correct classification.
items.fact: Fitch cut France from AA- to A+ on 12 Sep 2025 (stable outlook); S&P cut France from AA- to A+ on 17-18 Oct 2025 (stable outlook); Moody's affirmed Aa3 but moved to Negative outlook on 24 Oct 2025. Sources: Bloomberg/Fitch 12 Sep 2025; EDHEC/S&P 17 Oct 2025; Moody's press release ratings.moodys.com/ratings-news/453186, 24 Oct 2025.

items.label: USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
items.rationale: The rubric asks for the latest IMF COFER USD share of allocated global FX reserves as a percent. The most recent published figure is 56.77% for 2025Q4, released by the IMF on 2026-03-27, which maps directly and mechanically to the indicator. No Q1 2026 COFER release has been detected as of the search date.
items.fact: IMF COFER Data Brief (published 2026-03-27, data.imf.org/en/news/imf%20data%20brief%20march%2027): 'The share of US dollar holdings decreased to 56.77 percent in 2025Q4, from 56.93 percent in 2025Q3.' Total FX reserves stood at $13.14 trillion. This is the most recent official COFER release as of 2026-06-30; Q1 2026 data is not yet published (COFER runs with ~3-month lag, next release expected late June/early July 2026).

items.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
items.rationale: The rubric requires the signed YoY figure exactly as published by NBS; +3.9% is the most recent official NBS print (May 2026, released 2026-06-11), confirmed by CNBC and TradingEconomics. A +3.9% reading is a large positive move — the highest since July 2022 — signalling a meaningful cost-push input-price shock that could export inflationary pressure to trading partners.
items.fact: NBS official press release (https://www.stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202606/t20260611_1963929.html, released 2026-06-11): 'In May 2026, China's producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year and 0.5% month on month.'

items.label: Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)
items.rationale: Multiple authoritative sources (Benchmark Minerals, IEA, S&P Global/Project Blue) converge on a 4-6x ex-China premium for heavy rare earth oxides (dysprosium, terbium) vs. Chinese domestic prices in 2026; a central estimate of 4.5x reflects the Benchmark 4.4x anchor for 2025 escalating modestly into mid-2026 but capped below the 6x upper-bound cited for European peak pricing.
items.fact: Benchmark Minerals (March 24, 2026) reported dysprosium oxide CIF North America at 4.4x EXW China in 2025; IEA corroborates 'up to six times' for European buyers post-April 2025 controls; Investing News Network analysts (April 2026) cite '4-6x'; Metal Tech News (Feb 25, 2026) citing Strategic Metals Invest/TRADIUM data states Western dysprosium/terbium prices are 'roughly 20% of the prices quoted in Western markets' (implying ~5x). China domestic terbium: ~$970/kg (SMM, June 2026); FOB $1,406/kg (1.45x domestic), but true CIF Western market price substantially higher per Benchmark/IEA.

items.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The Fujian carrier transit and ongoing CCG grey-zone incursions represent assertive but non-kinetic PLA activity consistent with rubric level 1 (ELEVATED). No actual blockade, kinetic clash, or full mobilization has been reported by any authoritative source through June 30, 2026, precluding a level 2 (CRISIS) rating.
items.fact: PLAN carrier CNS Fujian transited the Taiwan Strait southbound on ~June 24, 2026; PLA MND spokesperson confirmed it as 'routine training.' No new large-scale drill announced in June 2026. Most recent major exercise was Justice Mission 2025 (Dec 29-30, 2025), a blockade-rehearsal involving 60 aircraft sorties, live rocket fire into Taiwan's contiguous zone, and CCG encirclement patrols. ISW/AEI China & Taiwan Update (June 26, 2026) reports continued grey-zone CCG incursions and maritime law enforcement operations east of Taiwan, but no kinetic clash or actual blockade. Sources: USNI News Western Pacific Pulse, June 26, 2026 (https://news.usni.org/2026/06/26/usni-news-western-pacific-pulse-june-26-2026); AEI/ISW China & Taiwan Update, June 26, 2026 (https://www.aei.org/articles/china-taiwan-update-june-26-2026/); Defense News, January 8, 2026 (https://www.defensenews.com/global/asia-pacific/2026/01/08/chinese-drill-near-taiwan-seen-as-test-run-for-blockade-message-to-us/).

items.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
items.rationale: Two named US rare-earth producers (MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc.) appear on MOFCOM's export-control list as of 22 June 2026, satisfying rubric level 1 (entity-specific). The listing covers 10 named firms and is explicitly characterised by multiple independent sources — including Benchmark Minerals and SMM — as entity-specific rather than a blanket or sector-wide restriction, so level 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: MOFCOM Announcement No. 23 of 2026 (effective 22 June 2026) added 10 named US entities to China's export-control list, including MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. — the two principal US domestic rare-earth producers. The listing bars Chinese exporters from supplying dual-use items to those firms and prohibits any party globally from transferring China-origin dual-use goods to them. The restriction is entity-specific: it names 10 firms and does not impose a categorical ban on the US rare-earth sector as a class. Market signal corroboration: share prices of both firms were roughly flat-to-modestly-higher on the day of the announcement (per Reuters/Bloomberg), consistent with entity-specific rather than sector-wide disruption. Sources: MOFCOM Announcement No. 23/2026 as reported by Bloomberg (2026-06-22), Benchmark Minerals (2026-06-25), Yahoo Finance/AFP (2026-06-22), SMM/metal.com (2026-06-22).

items.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: MoM change is -0.2%, so not spiking (rubric 2 threshold not met), but the index remains at a multi-year high and is positive YoY, ruling out rubric 0 (which requires both MoM and YoY declines); rubric 1 (ELEVATED, MoM change <3%) applies.
items.fact: FAO Food Price Index averaged 130.8 points in May 2026, down 0.2 points (-0.2% MoM) from a revised April level of 131.0 (highest since Jan 2023), but still above year-ago levels; June 2026 release is scheduled 3 July 2026. Source: fao.org/worldfoodsituation/foodpricesindex, released 5 June 2026.

flags.name: Redemption gates widespread, but HY spreads benign
flags.message: Large-fund redemption gates are rated WIDESPREAD (2) but high-yield spreads are benign. Broad gating usually coincides with visible credit stress; confirm the gates are systemic, not a couple of isolated funds.

indicators.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)

s1_basis_trade.label: Hidden leverage in US government bonds
s1_basis_trade.gist: Hedge funds borrow heavily to trade US government bonds; if that trade unwinds fast it can freeze the market everyone treats as safe.

s2_japan.label: Japan's rates and the global cash carry trade
s2_japan.gist: Cheap Japanese money has funded bets worldwide for years; as Japan's interest rates rise, pulling that money home can ripple through global markets.

s3_private_credit.label: Private lending you can't see into
s3_private_credit.gist: A fast-growing pile of loans sits outside the banks with little public disclosure, so stress there is hard to spot until it is already large.

s4_dev_debt.label: Debt stress in developing countries
s4_dev_debt.gist: Many poorer nations owe more than they can comfortably repay; a wave of defaults can spread and pull in their lenders.

s5_china_deflation.label: China's prices spilling into the world
s5_china_deflation.gist: Big swings in China's factory prices - falling or rising - get exported to everyone else as cheaper goods or higher input costs.

s6_rare_earths.label: Rare-earth minerals used as a weapon
s6_rare_earths.gist: China controls most of the world's rare-earth processing and is now restricting it, squeezing US defense and high-tech manufacturing.

s7_stablecoins.label: Crypto dollars and US government debt
s7_stablecoins.gist: Stablecoins must hold US Treasuries as backing, so their rapid growth - or a sudden wobble - now feeds straight back into the government-debt market.

s8_ai_capex.label: The AI spending boom
s8_ai_capex.gist: Enormous borrowing to build AI data centers is racing ahead of the revenue meant to justify it, concentrating the risk in a few giant companies.

s9_cre.label: Commercial real estate and small banks
s9_cre.gist: Half-empty offices and loans coming due strain the regional banks that hold them - a slow-burning credit risk.

s10_france.label: France and eurozone stability
s10_france.gist: A politically gridlocked, heavily indebted France is the soft spot that could reopen broader euro-area stress.

s11_consumer_debt.label: Household debt strain
s11_consumer_debt.gist: Rising late payments on car loans and buy-now-pay-later show households stretching - an early warning on consumer health.

s12_gold_dedollar.label: Gold and moving away from the dollar
s12_gold_dedollar.gist: Central banks stockpiling gold and building non-dollar payment systems slowly chip away at the dollar's dominance.

s13_insurance.label: Insurers pulling back
s13_insurance.gist: As disaster losses climb, insurers raise prices or quit whole regions, leaving homes and assets exposed.

s14_pensions.label: Pensions and an aging population
s14_pensions.gist: Aging populations and underfunded pensions strain public budgets; leveraged pension funds can also break in a sudden rate shock, as the UK saw in 2022.

s15_ai_power.label: AI's strain on the power grid
s15_ai_power.gist: AI data centers need more electricity than the grid can quickly supply, triggering curtailments, building moratoria, and multi-year connection queues.

s16_copper.label: Copper for electrification
s16_copper.gist: Electrifying everything needs far more copper than mines can deliver, and major mine outages are tightening supply further.

s17_water.label: Water as a hidden constraint
s17_water.gist: Droughts and shrinking reservoirs threaten chip factories, farms, and power generation - an underpriced input with no substitute.

s18_food_fertilizer.label: Food and fertilizer supply
s18_food_fertilizer.gist: Export curbs and shipping disruptions to fertilizer raise the risk of higher food prices down the line.

s19_lithography.label: The chipmaking chokepoint
s19_lithography.gist: The most advanced chips depend on one Dutch tool-maker and Taiwan's factories; any disruption there hits the entire tech economy.

s20_sea_lanes.label: Shipping lanes and undersea cables
s20_sea_lanes.gist: Key straits like Hormuz and the undersea cables carrying the internet are vulnerable chokepoints for global trade and data.

s21_clearinghouses.label: The financial system's plumbing
s21_clearinghouses.gist: Clearinghouses sit at the center of every market; if one seized up under stress, it would jam trading everywhere at once.
